question: A thermocline is a water layer where:

options.a: Salinity changes rapidly with depth
options.b: Water becomes rapidly hot
options.c: All answers are correct
options.d: Temperature changes rapidly

explanation: Thermocline = layer where temperature changes rapidly with depth (halocline = salinity).
source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

question: What are components of the blood?

options.a: Red blood cells
options.b: White blood cells and platelets
options.c: Water, enzymes, hormones, and clotting factors
options.d: All answers are correct

explanation: Blood contains red and white cells, platelets, plus plasma (water, enzymes, hormones, clotting factors) - all components.
source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

question: A diver has a mass of 87 kilograms when neutrally buoyant in seawater. To be neutrally buoyant in fresh water, they must:

options.a: Add weight
options.b: Use the same weight
options.c: Remove weight
options.d: Use different equipment

explanation: Fresh water is less dense than seawater, so it provides less buoyant support; the diver must remove weight.
source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

question: Signs and symptoms of inert gas narcosis include:

options.a: All answers are correct
options.b: Euphoria
options.c: Poor memory
options.d: Increased response times

explanation: Inert gas narcosis causes euphoria, impaired memory, and slowed responses - all are symptoms.
source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

question: When can signs of decompression sickness appear?

options.a: Immediately upon surfacing
options.b: All answers are correct
options.c: Within 15 minutes of a dive
options.d: Within 12 hours of a dive

explanation: DCS signs can appear immediately on surfacing, within 15 minutes, or up to 12+ hours later - all timeframes possible.
source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

question: Salinity increases in regions affected by:

options.a: Low rainfall and high evaporation
options.b: High rainfall and low evaporation
options.c: Especially salty rocks
options.d: Ice melt

explanation: Low rainfall plus high evaporation concentrates dissolved salts, increasing salinity.
source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

question: Regulator icing is caused by the:

options.a: Rapid compression of breathing gas
options.b: Swimming around icebergs
options.c: Rapid expansion of breathing gas
options.d: Thin regulator materials

explanation: Rapid expansion of breathing gas at the first/second stage cools moisture and can freeze the regulator.
source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

question: Signs and symptoms of central nervous system oxygen toxicity include:

options.a: Coughing and a burning sensation in the lungs
options.b: Cherry red lips and coughing
options.c: Numbness and tingling
options.d: Convulsions and twitching

explanation: CNS oxygen toxicity classic signs include convulsions and twitching (the 'C' in ConVENTID).
source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

question: Where does gas exchange take place in the lungs?

options.a: Through pulmonary capillaries in the alveoli
options.b: Through pulmonary capillaries in the trachea
options.c: Through arteries in the vena cava
options.d: All answers are correct

explanation: Gas exchange occurs across pulmonary capillaries surrounding the alveoli.
source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

question: What is a function of the cardiovascular system?

options.a: To transport carbon dioxide from the lungs to the body, and transport oxygen back to the lungs
options.b: To keep blood vessels vasodilated
options.c: To transport oxygen from the lungs to the body, and transport carbon dioxide back to the lungs
options.d: To decompress adipose tissue

explanation: The cardiovascular system transports oxygen from lungs to body tissues and CO2 back to lungs for exhalation.
source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

question: Neoprene provides insulation because it contains:

options.a: Closed cells of gas
options.b: Heated filaments
options.c: Dissolved gas
options.d: Thick layers

explanation: Neoprene insulates because of closed gas-filled cells; this is also why it compresses with depth.
source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

question: When is a gas supersaturated?

options.a: When there is less gas in solution than the substance can hold at equilibrium
options.b: When the pressure of the gases in and out of solution become equal
options.c: When the pressure of the gas in solution exceeds the pressure of the gas out of solution
options.d: When it is full of gas

explanation: A gas is supersaturated when its dissolved-pressure exceeds the surrounding (out-of-solution) pressure.
source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

question: When does a diver deploy a delayed surface marker buoy?

options.a: To mark the ascent or at the end of the dive
options.b: Throughout the dive
options.c: To mark the descent at the beginning of the dive
options.d: All answers are correct

explanation: A delayed SMB is deployed at the start of the ascent (or end of dive) to mark position for the boat.
source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

question: What are two potential hazards of breathing higher concentrations of oxygen?

options.a: Histotoxic hypoxia and anemic hypoxia
options.b: Hyperventilation and pulmonary oxygen toxicity
options.c: Central nervous system oxygen toxicity and pulmonary oxygen toxicity
options.d: Hemoglobin dysfunction and carbon monoxide poisoning

explanation: Breathing high pO2 risks both CNS and pulmonary oxygen toxicity.
source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

question: The term “decompression illness" covers which diving- related disorders?

options.a: Type I and Type II decompression sickness and hyperoxia
options.b: Subcutaneous emphysema and narcosis
options.c: Hypercapnia and arterial gas embolism
options.d: Decompression sickness, lung overexpansion, and arterial gas embolism

explanation: Decompression illness umbrella covers DCS, lung overexpansion injuries, and arterial gas embolism.
source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

question: Why does hypothermia increase the risk of decompression sickness?

options.a: The dilation of blood vessels reduces circulation
options.b: The dilation of blood vessels increases heat absorption
options.c: The constriction of blood vessels reduces circulation
options.d: The constriction of blood vessels increases blood flow

explanation: Hypothermia constricts peripheral blood vessels, reducing circulation and impairing inert-gas elimination.
source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

question: What is plankton?

options.a: A diverse group of organisms that cannot swim effectively and drift with ocean currents
options.b: Microscopic bacteria only
options.c: Microscopic algae only
options.d: A group of protists

explanation: Plankton is a diverse group of drifting organisms that cannot swim effectively against currents.
source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

question: What are the main inflation mechanisms on a buoyancy compensator?

options.a: The power inflator and oral inflator
options.b: The power inflator and power deflator
options.c: The power inflator and dump valves
options.d: The oral inflator and exhaust button

explanation: A BC is inflated via the power inflator and the oral inflator (backup).
source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

question: What is a diver’s SAC rate if they dive for 10 minutes at a depth of 10 meters, and consume 50 bar from a 10-liter cylinder?

options.a: 50 liters per minute
options.b: 35 liters per minute
options.c: 10 liters per minute
options.d: 25 liters per minute

explanation: SAC: 50 bar x 10 L = 500 L over 10 min at 2 ATA = 25 L/min surface equivalent.
source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

question: How do colors change as a diver descends?

options.a: They fade rapidly
options.b: Red, orange and yellow lose their color quite shallow
options.c: On a deep dive in clear water divers will see blue, indigo, and violet
options.d: All answers are correct

explanation: Colors fade rapidly with depth - red/orange/yellow first, then on deep clear dives blue/indigo/violet remain.
source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

question: Which of the following statements about steel cylinders is true?

options.a: They are made of denser and stronger material than aluminum
options.b: They are generally more negatively or neutrally buoyant compared to aluminum cylinders
options.c: All answers are correct
options.d: They may be preferred when thicker wetsuits increase buoyancy

explanation: Steel is denser/stronger than aluminum, more negatively/neutrally buoyant, and preferred with thick wetsuits - all true.
source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

question: Gradient factors adjust the level of conservatism by:

options.a: Increasing the maximum tolerated supersaturation in the tissues compared to Bühlmann’s original values
options.b: Adjusting the temperature gradient in water
options.c: Lowering the maximum tolerated supersaturation in the tissues compared to Bühlmann’s original values
options.d: Adjusting the diver’s inert gas absorption

explanation: Gradient factors lower the maximum tolerated supersaturation vs. Buhlmann original M-values to add conservatism.
source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

question: Which of the following best describes the relationship between wind and wave formation?

options.a: Wind creates waves by increasing ocean salinity
options.b: Wind has little effect on ocean surface conditions
options.c: Wind transfers energy to the water’s surface, generating waves
options.d: Wind causes water to cool, preventing waves from forming

explanation: Wind transfers energy to the water surface, generating waves.
source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

question: During transport, cylinders should be:

options.a: Completely empty
options.b: Cooled down
options.c: Properly secured
options.d: All answers are correct

explanation: Cylinders being transported should be properly secured to prevent movement and damage.
source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

question: Signs and symptoms of severe hypothermia can include:

options.a: Uncontrollable shivering, decreased heart and breathing rate, and unconsciousness
options.b: Absence of shivering, decreased heart and breathing rate, and unconsciousness
options.c: A core temperature between about 32–35°C
options.d: Uncontrollable shivering, increased heart and breathing rate, and unconsciousness

explanation: Severe hypothermia: shivering stops, heart and breathing rate decrease, and unconsciousness occurs.
source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

question: What is the definition of “half-time" ?

options.a: The number of minutes required for a body tissue to absorb half the total amount of an inert gas that it would contain if it were completely saturated at a specific depth or partial pressure
options.b: The number of minutes required for a body tissue to absorb twice the total amount of an inert gas that it would contain if it were completely saturated at a specific depth or partial pressure
options.c: The number of minutes required for a body tissue to eliminate the nitrogen it contains
options.d: The number of minutes required for a diver complete half their dive

explanation: Half-time = minutes for a tissue to absorb half of the inert gas it would hold at full saturation for that pressure.
source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

question: What is the basic purpose of the first and second stages?

options.a: The first stage reduces high- pressure cylinder gas to intermediate pressure
options.b: The second stage reduces intermediate-pressure gas to ambient pressure
options.c: All answers are correct
options.d: First- and second-stage valves open and close according to demand

explanation: First stage: HP -> IP. Second stage: IP -> ambient on demand. Valves open/close per demand. All correct.
source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

question: Multi-level diving complicates decompression theory because:

options.a: The diver is exposed to reduced atmospheric pressure
options.b: The drop in pressure can release nitrogen from solution
options.c: The diver’s tissues continually saturate with nitrogen throughout the dive
options.d: The diver’s tissues saturate and desaturate throughout the dive

explanation: In multilevel dives, tissues both saturate (deeper) and desaturate (shallower) throughout the dive, complicating modeling.
source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

question: Which of the following statements about wetsuit compression is true?

options.a: It decreases positive buoyancy at depth
options.b: Neoprene compresses under pressure
options.c: Insulation is reduced
options.d: All answers are correct

explanation: With depth, neoprene compresses, insulation drops, and positive buoyancy decreases - all true.
source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

question: What is the no- decompression limit based on?

options.a: Dive depth, duration, surface interval, and tissue nitrogen absorption
options.b: Oxygen levels in the atmosphere
options.c: Time spent at maximum depth
options.d: The number of required decompression stops

explanation: NDL is computed from depth, duration, surface interval, and the resulting tissue nitrogen loading.
source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

question: Which of the following statements about evaporative heat loss is true?

options.a: When water evaporates it takes heat energy away from the body
options.b: Divers can experience evaporative heat loss when water evaporates from their skin
options.c: Dry breathing gas can cause evaporative heat loss as mucus linings dry out in the lungs
options.d: All answers are correct

explanation: Evaporation removes heat from skin and from drying mucus in the lungs - all answers describe true mechanisms.
source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

question: What does it mean if a body tissue is poorly-perfused?

options.a: It eliminates gas more slowly
options.b: It has a good blood supply
options.c: It absorbs gas quickly
options.d: It eliminates gas quickly

explanation: Poorly-perfused tissues have poor blood supply and therefore eliminate (and absorb) inert gas slowly.
source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

question: What preventative measures can help prevent barotrauma?

options.a: Equalize the ears, sinuses and lungs frequently
options.b: Avoid rapid pressure changes
options.c: All answers are correct
options.d: Never hold the breath

explanation: Equalize early/often, avoid rapid pressure changes, and never hold the breath - all help prevent barotrauma.
source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

question: What are the two different types of valve connections?

options.a: Yoke and DIN connections
options.b: Aluminum and steel connections
options.c: DIN and steel connections
options.d: Brass and steel connections

explanation: The two valve connection standards are yoke (international/A-clamp) and DIN.
source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

question: When wetsuits get thicker, buoyancy:

options.a: Decreases
options.b: Stays the same
options.c: Increases
options.d: Is not a problem

explanation: Thicker wetsuits contain more closed gas cells, which increases buoyancy (and requires more lead).
source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

question: What is the mammalian dive reflex?

options.a: A series of psychological changes that result from seeing mammals in the water
options.b: A series of physiological changes that result from breath-holds, especially during immersion
options.c: A reflex that makes humans want to dive
options.d: A series of physiological changes that result from tachypnea during a dive

explanation: The mammalian dive reflex is a set of physiological changes (bradycardia, vasoconstriction) triggered by breath-hold and facial immersion.
source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

question: Understanding how gas solubility relates to pressure helps divers understand:

options.a: How far they can push their limits
options.b: How gases expand and compress in equipment
options.c: How much gas they can safely breathe on a dive
options.d: How gases are absorbed into body tissues and the risks associated with decompression illness

explanation: Gas-solubility/pressure relationships explain inert-gas absorption in tissues and the resulting decompression risks.
source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

question: What is the established treatment for decompression illness?

options.a: Recompression underwater
options.b: Recompression in a hyperbaric chamber
options.c: No treatment, the diver must wait to off-gas
options.d: Recompression on the boat

explanation: Definitive DCI treatment is recompression in a hyperbaric chamber with oxygen.
source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

question: What is hypothermia?

options.a: A medical condition where the body loses heat faster than it can produce heat.
options.b: A medical condition where the body produces or absorbs more heat than it can dissipate.
options.c: A medical condition caused by freezing of the skin and underlying tissues.
options.d: A medical condition where the body produces too much carbon dioxide.

explanation: Hypothermia = body loses heat faster than it can produce it.
source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

question: Which of the following statements is true about cylinder care and maintenance?

options.a: Cylinders should be inspected frequently for signs of damage or corrosion
options.b: All answers are correct
options.c: Cylinders should be kept with a minimum gas pressure of twenty to fifty bar
options.d: Valves used with aluminum cylinders must be periodically lubricated

explanation: Inspect for damage/corrosion, keep 20-50 bar minimum residual pressure, lubricate aluminum-cylinder valves periodically - all true.
source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

question: What happens when a wave reaches the surf zone?

options.a: The wave speeds up and continues moving offshore
options.b: The wave completely stops moving forward
options.c: The wave slows down, increases in height, and breaks
options.d: The wave becomes a tidal bore and moves inland

explanation: In the surf zone the wave slows, builds height, then breaks.
source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

question: Oxygen-compatible equipment is equipment that is:

options.a: Needs adapting for use with nitrox
options.b: All equipment is oxygen- compatible
options.c: No answers are correct
options.d: Manufactured ready for use with nitrox

explanation: Oxygen-compatible equipment is manufactured ready for use with nitrox (cleaned/built for elevated O2).
source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

question: How might gas bubbles enter arterial blood vessels?

options.a: Through a cut in the diver’s skin
options.b: Pulmonary barotrauma or shunting through a patent foramen ovale
options.c: Due to excessive vasoconstriction
options.d: Because of a tight wetsuit

explanation: Arterial bubbles enter via pulmonary barotrauma or by shunting through a patent foramen ovale (PFO).
source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

question: What is an advantage of using a watch-style computer as a dive computer?

options.a: They do not require additional hoses or consoles
options.b: They are designed to be worn on the wrist like a regular watch
options.c: All answers are correct
options.d: They are lightweight and streamlined

explanation: Watch-style computers are wrist-worn, lightweight, streamlined, and need no extra hose/console - all advantages.
source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

question: What is a pressure gradient?

options.a: The force resulting from equal pressures
options.b: The force resulting from a difference in pressure
options.c: The force of a substance of higher pressure
options.d: The difference in concentrations within a substance or across two areas

explanation: A pressure gradient is the force resulting from a difference in pressure between two regions.
source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

question: Styles of dive flag include:

options.a: The Alpha flag and national flag
options.b: The Alpha flag and recreational diver flag
options.c: The recreational diver flag and boat flag
options.d: The recreational diver flag and national flag

explanation: Recreational diver flag (red with white diagonal stripe) and Alpha flag (international maritime) are the two dive-flag styles.
source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

question: What actions can divers take to reduce the risks of hyperthermia?

options.a: All answers are correct
options.b: Drink plenty of fluids
options.c: Don wetsuits just before the dive
options.d: Minimize effort and exertion

explanation: Hydrate, don wetsuits just before entry, and minimize exertion - all reduce hyperthermia risk.
source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

question: Why should divers carry multiple signaling devices?

options.a: To be prepared for different emergency situations
options.b: Carrying more devices lessens the chance of losing any single one
options.c: Device diversity helps divers maintain buoyancy control
options.d: Having multiple devices encourages faster underwater navigation

explanation: Multiple signaling devices cover varied emergencies and provide redundancy if one is lost.
source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

question: What are signs and symptoms of sinus barotrauma?

options.a: Pain behind the eyes, teeth, or cheekbones
options.b: Nasal congestion or bleeding
options.c: All answers are correct
options.d: Headache

explanation: Sinus barotrauma symptoms include pain behind eyes/teeth/cheekbones, congestion or bleeding, and headache - all of the above.
source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

question: Archimedes’ Principle states that:

options.a: The sum of partial pressures in a gas mixture is equal to the total gas pressure
options.b: The amount of a gas dissolved in a liquid is proportional to its partial pressure
options.c: An object placed in a fluid will be depressed by a force equal to the weight of the fluid it displaces
options.d: An object placed in a fluid will be buoyed up by a force equal to the weight of the fluid it displaces

explanation: Archimedes: an object in a fluid is buoyed up by a force equal to the weight of the displaced fluid.
source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

question: How does an increase in gas pressure influence tissue saturation?

options.a: It increases tissue saturation
options.b: It decreases tissue saturation
options.c: It causes tissue supersaturation
options.d: It saturates tissues with microbubbles

explanation: Higher ambient gas pressure raises the partial pressure gradient driving inert gas into tissues, increasing saturation.
source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

question: Which of the following statements about carbon dioxide is true?

options.a: All answers are correct
options.b: It is a natural byproduct of the body's metabolism
options.c: It should be eliminated from breathing mixtures
options.d: High concentrations will disrupt normal bodily functions

explanation: CO2 is a metabolic byproduct, must be eliminated from breathing gas, and high levels disrupt normal function - all true.
source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

question: The "apparent mass" (the buoyancy) of an object can be calculated:

options.a: By adding the weight of the displaced water to the mass of the immersed object
options.b: By subtracting the mass of the displaced water from the mass of the immersed object
options.c: By testing the mass of the object using scales in water
options.d: By weighing it down and seeing if it will float

explanation: Apparent mass = mass of object minus mass of displaced water (Archimedes).
source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

question: What are parts of the ear?

options.a: The outer ear, eardrum, middle ear, ossicles, Eustachian tube, and bronchioles
options.b: The outer ear, eardrum, middle ear, ossicles, inner ear, round and oval windows, and Eustachian tube
options.c: The outer ear, eardrum, middle ear, inner ear, Eustachian tube, and maxillary sinuses
options.d: The earlobe, middle ear, inner ear, and Valsalva

explanation: Ear anatomy: outer ear, eardrum, middle ear with ossicles, inner ear with round/oval windows, and the Eustachian tube.
source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

question: What should divers do if they are caught in a strong current?

options.a: Swim directly against the current
options.b: Fight the current for as long as they can
options.c: Add more weight so they can stay in one place
options.d: Swim parallel/at an angle to the shore or drift with the current and wait to be picked up

explanation: Don't fight a current; swim parallel/at an angle to shore or drift and signal for pickup.
source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

question: The absolute pressure at a depth of fifty meters:

options.a: 5.0 bar
options.b: 5.1 bar
options.c: 5.6 bar
options.d: 6.0 bar

explanation: Absolute pressure = 1 bar (surface) + 5 bar (50 m water) = 6.0 bar.
source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

question: Which blood vessels are used for gas exchange in the body?

options.a: The aorta
options.b: Arteries
options.c: The vena cava
options.d: Capillaries

explanation: Capillaries are the thin-walled vessels where gas, nutrient, and waste exchange occurs at the tissues and lungs.
source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

question: A physiological condition called a squeeze occurs when:

options.a: The pressure on the inside of an air space is greater than the external pressure
options.b: The pressure on the outside of an closed air space is greater than the internal pressure
options.c: The pressure on the inside of an air space is increasing to equalize with the external pressure
options.d: None of the answers are correct

explanation: A squeeze (descent barotrauma) occurs when external pressure exceeds internal pressure of a closed air space.
source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

question: How often should equipment be serviced?

options.a: Once a year
options.b: At least annually and according to manufacturer instructions
options.c: Once a week and according to manufacturer instructions
options.d: No answers are correct

explanation: Service equipment at least annually and follow the manufacturer's specific instructions/intervals.
source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

question: Hypoxia is a condition resulting from:

options.a: Short breath-holds
options.b: Excess oxygen in the body tissues
options.c: Lack of oxygen in the body
options.d: Elevated carbon dioxide levels in the blood

explanation: Hypoxia = insufficient oxygen reaching the body tissues.
source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

question: What is inert gas narcosis?

options.a: Physical and mental disturbance caused by breathing gas at a reduced pressure
options.b: A disturbance caused by divers drinking too many martinis
options.c: The reduced effects of nitrogen
options.d: Physical and mental disturbance caused by breathing gas at an elevated pressure

explanation: Inert gas narcosis is physical and mental impairment from breathing inert gases (esp. nitrogen) at elevated partial pressures.
source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

question: Why should divers carry a cutting device?

options.a: To respond to underwater entanglements hazards
options.b: To carve navigational markers into underwater rocks
options.c: To signal other divers through metal clanging
options.d: To hunt underwater creatures more effectively

explanation: A cutting device is essential for freeing yourself or your buddy from line/net entanglements.
source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

question: What factors can increase the risk of immersion pulmonary edema?

options.a: Pre-existing cardiac problems
options.b: Diving in cold water and inspiring against a negative pressure
options.c: Exertion and stress
options.d: All answers are correct

explanation: IPE risk factors include cardiac history, cold water with negative inspiratory pressure, and exertion/stress - all of the above.
source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

question: Which of the following statements about nitrox dive computers is true?

options.a: Most standard dive computers are nitrox-capable
options.b: Nitrox computers can be used for air dives
options.c: Nitrox computers calculate data based on increased oxygen levels
options.d: All answers are correct

explanation: Nitrox computers can also be used on air (set FO2 = 21%); not all dive computers are nitrox-capable.
source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

question: Which if the following statements about argon is true?

options.a: It is half as narcotic as nitrogen and used as a diluent gas
options.b: It has a low density and half the insulating capacity of air
options.c: It is more narcotic than nitrogen and not used as a breathing gas
options.d: It is expensive and not used as a dry suit inflation gas

explanation: Argon has roughly half the narcotic potency of nitrogen; it is sometimes used as a diluent or drysuit-inflation gas.
source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

question: Hypercapnia can be prevented:

options.a: By breathing in a controlled manner
options.b: By minimizing workload at depth
options.c: With proper equipment maintenance
options.d: All answers are correct

explanation: Controlled breathing, low workload, and well-maintained gear all help prevent CO2 buildup (hypercapnia).
source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

question: What are microbubbles?

options.a: Small gas bubbles that a diver breathes out through their regulator
options.b: Tiny inert gas bubbles that form in a diver's bloodstream and tissues
options.c: Tiny inert gas bubbles that dissolve in a diver's bloodstream and tissues
options.d: Small gas bubbles that form only in well-perfused tissues

explanation: Microbubbles are tiny inert-gas bubbles that form in the bloodstream and tissues during/after ascent.
source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

question: At what partial pressure of oxygen can hypoxia occur?
explanation: Hypoxia can occur at pO2 below approximately 0.16 bar.
source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

question: Understanding the period of a wave helps divers:

options.a: Calculate underwater plant growth rates
options.b: Time their movements and navigate safely
options.c: All answers are correct
options.d: Manage their depth more accurately

explanation: Wave period helps divers time entries, exits, and movements to navigate safely through surf.
source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

question: Which of the following statements about carbon monoxide is true?

options.a: It is harmless and can be breathed by divers
options.b: It is highly toxic
options.c: It is not produced by the burning of fuel and oil
options.d: It is easily seen, smelled, and tasted

explanation: Carbon monoxide is highly toxic, colorless, odorless, and tasteless - it binds hemoglobin far more strongly than oxygen.
source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

question: How can divers minimize negative interactions with marine life?

options.a: Move cautiously, avoid sudden movements, maintain buoyancy, and avoid covered areas
options.b: Observe, but do not touch or bait animals
options.c: All answers are correct
options.d: Wear appropriate exposure suits and follow local regulations

explanation: Cautious movement, observe-don't-touch, proper exposure protection and following local rules - all minimize negative interactions.
source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

question: Which of the following statements about nitrogen is true?

options.a: It is not metabolized by the body but can diffuse in and out of body tissues
options.b: Dissolved nitrogen can come out of solution and increase the risk of decompression sickness
options.c: It can cause narcosis at high partial pressures
options.d: All answers are correct

explanation: Nitrogen is inert (not metabolized) but diffuses in/out of tissues, can come out of solution causing DCS, and is narcotic at high pp - all true.
source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

question: What is a reverse block?

options.a: A squeeze in reverse that happens when compressing gas becomes trapped on descent
options.b: A blockage in the middle ear
options.c: A squeeze in reverse that happens when expanding gas becomes trapped on ascent
options.d: A arterial blockage caused by gas bubbles

explanation: Reverse block: expanding gas trapped in an air space (sinus, ear) on ascent unable to vent, causing pain/barotrauma.
source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

question: How does a gas-integrated dive computer help divers manage their gas?

options.a: By automatically adjusting the diver's breathing rate
options.b: All answers are correct
options.c: By storing historical data about the diver's gas consumption
options.d: By monitoring and displaying the remaining gas supply in real- time

explanation: Gas-integrated computers monitor cylinder pressure and display remaining gas in real time (and often calculate gas time).
source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

question: Where does the greatest expansion or compression per meter occur?

options.a: 30–40 meters
options.b: 0–10 meters
options.c: 10–20 meters
options.d: 20–30 meters

explanation: Boyle's law: greatest relative volume change occurs near the surface (0-10 m), where pressure doubles.
source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

question: How might divers be at risk of hyperthermia?

options.a: All answers are correct
options.b: They overheat by wearing thermal insulation in warm environments
options.c: Through overexertion
options.d: Through inadequate hydration

explanation: Wearing thermal insulation in warm conditions, overexertion, and inadequate hydration all raise hyperthermia risk.
source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

question: Factors that might affect a diver's breathing gas consumption include:

options.a: Depth, temperature, and environmental conditions
options.b: All answers are correct
options.c: Gender, age, body weight, physiology
options.d: Mental and physical exertion, experience, and confidence

explanation: Depth/temperature/environment, personal physiology, and mental/physical state all affect gas consumption - all of the above.
source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

question: What is the immediate care in a suspected decompression illness emergency?

options.a: All answers are correct
options.b: Seek medical assistance
options.c: Administer 100% oxygen
options.d: Monitor vital signs and be ready to perform CPR

explanation: Immediate DCI care: seek medical help, give 100% O2, monitor vitals and be ready for CPR - all correct.
source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

question: A dive computer algorithm considers _____ to provide conservative estimates.

options.a: Water temperature, weather, and visibility
options.b: Type of marine life present, and duration of dive
options.c: All answers are correct
options.d: Dive profile, depth, time, surface interval, and gas mixture

explanation: Algorithms use dive profile, depth, time, surface interval, and gas mixture to compute conservative decompression estimates.
source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

question: How does hyperventilation possibly cause hypoxia?

options.a: It massively increases the amount of oxygen a diver inhales
options.b: It causes an imbalance between oxygen intake and carbon dioxide levels
options.c: It decreases hemoglobin levels
options.d: It decreases blood carbon monoxide levels

explanation: Hyperventilation lowers CO2, removing the breathing trigger; the diver may then black out from hypoxia before feeling air hunger.
source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

question: Which features distinguish a reel from a spool?

options.a: A spool is preferred for longer deployments
options.b: A reel has a handle, a brake or locking mechanism, and a winding knob
options.c: All answers are correct
options.d: A reel is always smaller and lighter

explanation: A reel has a handle, a brake/locking mechanism, and a winding knob - distinguishing it from a simple spool.
source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

question: What areas of the body are typically affected by Type II decompression sickness?

options.a: The central nervous system
options.b: The inner ear
options.c: The cardiorespiratory system
options.d: All answers are correct

explanation: Type II (serious) DCS can affect the CNS, inner ear, and cardiorespiratory system - all of the above.
source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

question: A diver experiencing pain in their ears or sinuses while descending should:

options.a: Ascend until the pressure is relieved and then attempt equalization
options.b: Return to the surface and re-initiate the descent
options.c: Ascend 2 meters and force equalization
options.d: Stop and wait until the discomfort subsides

explanation: If pain develops on descent, ascend slightly until pressure is relieved, then equalize before continuing.
source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

question: What is the partial pressure of a gas?

options.a: The sum of all the pressures of gases in a mixture
options.b: The individual pressure of a gas in a mixture of gases
options.c: The total pressure of the gas minus the gas that a diver breathes
options.d: The pressure of argon in a mixture

explanation: Partial pressure = the individual pressure exerted by one gas component in a mixture (Dalton's Law).
source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

question: Which gas law says that pressure and volume are inversely related?

options.a: Dalton's Law
options.b: Henry's Law
options.c: Charles' Law
options.d: Boyle's Law

explanation: Boyle's Law: at constant temperature, pressure and volume of a gas are inversely proportional (P1V1 = P2V2).
source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

question: What is the purpose of performing a safety stop during ascent?

options.a: To maintain an ascent rate of 9 meters per minute
options.b: To regulate off-gassing by making predetermined stop at a progressively shallower depth
options.c: To prevent the formation of bubbles as the gases separate out of solution
options.d: To develop a dissolved nitrogen tension higher than the surrounding pressure

explanation: Safety stops regulate off-gassing by pausing at progressively shallower depths to allow controlled inert gas elimination.
source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

question: What are the three main groups of fishes?

options.a: Bony fishes, cartilaginous fishes, and orca
options.b: Cartilaginous fishes, bony fishes, and carnivorous fishes
options.c: All answers are correct
options.d: Bony fishes, cartilaginous fishes, and jawless fishes

explanation: The three main groups of fishes are bony fishes (Osteichthyes), cartilaginous fishes (Chondrichthyes), and jawless fishes (Agnatha).
source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

question: How might helium be used in diving?

options.a: Specially trained divers use it in trimix and heliox gas mixtures to increase the effects of narcosis at depth
options.b: Specially trained divers use it in trimix and heliox gas mixtures to reduce the effects of narcosis at depth
options.c: Specially trained divers use it to inflate their dry suits for extra buoyancy
options.d: It is not used in diving

explanation: Helium is used in trimix/heliox to reduce narcosis at depth, since it is far less narcotic than nitrogen.
source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

question: What is the minimum gas required for a buddy pair with a surface air consumption of 18 liters per minute, if they want to dive to 33 meters with 12-liter cylinders, and have a 2 minutes of troubleshooting?

options.a: 36 bar
options.b: 53 bar
options.c: 60 bar
options.d: 78 bar

explanation: Minimum gas (rock-bottom) for buddy pair at 33 m (4.3 ATA), 18 L/min SAC, with troubleshooting and ascent allowance, in 12 L cylinders works out to about 78 bar.
source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

question: Plants and phytoplankton use carbon dioxide to:

options.a: Kill bacteria during chemosynthesis
options.b: Generate bubbles during photosynthesis
options.c: Decrease water salinity
options.d: Produce energy and release oxygen during photosynthesis

explanation: Photosynthesis: plants/phytoplankton use CO2 (and water + sunlight) to produce energy and release oxygen.
source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

question: Which predisposing factors can increase the risk of decompression sickness?

options.a: A high fitness level, being under the age of 40, and exposure to warm water
options.b: Being well-rested, having a healthy diet, and being an experienced diver
options.c: A low fitness level, being over the age of 50, and exposure to cold water
options.d: Using loose-fitting equipment, being dehydrated, and diving in clear water

explanation: Low fitness, older age, and cold-water exposure all increase DCS risk.
source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

question: Why is it important to maintain proper stop depth control during a dive?

options.a: To increase the time required to reduce nitrogen in the tissues
options.b: To prevent oxygen toxicity during ascent
options.c: To allow the tissues to efficiently absorb nitrogen
options.d: To maximize the pressure gradient available for off-gassing and reduce the risk of decompression sickness

explanation: Proper stop depth maximizes the off-gassing pressure gradient while keeping bubble growth controlled, reducing DCS risk.
source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

question: What is the primary function of seagrass beds in marine ecosystems?

options.a: They provide food and habitat for various marine species
options.b: They generate waves and strong currents
options.c: They prevent all sediment from settling on the seafloor
options.d: They stop seahorses being consumed by predators

explanation: Seagrass beds are primary habitat and food source for many marine species; they also stabilize sediment.
source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

question: A submersible pressure gauge is connected to the:

options.a: Regulator's low-pressure port
options.b: Regulator's high-pressure port
options.c: Power inflator
options.d: Bourdon tube

explanation: An SPG measures cylinder pressure and connects to the regulator's high-pressure (HP) port.
source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

question: What is an arterial gas embolism?

options.a: A bleed on the brain
options.b: The blockage of a vein by bubbles
options.c: Blue lips caused by low oxygen
options.d: The blockage of an arterial blood vessel by bubbles

explanation: Arterial gas embolism (AGE) = blockage of an artery by gas bubbles, often from pulmonary barotrauma.
source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

question: The M-value is the _____ value of nitrogen.

options.a: Maximum tolerated supersaturation
options.b: Minimum tolerated supersaturation
options.c: Maximum tolerated saturation
options.d: Minimum tolerated saturation

explanation: M-value = maximum tolerated tissue supersaturation (per tissue compartment) before symptoms become likely.
source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

question: What are trim weights?

options.a: Extra weights carried for emergencies
options.b: Adjustable weights that help the diver achieve proper trim
options.c: Weights used only at the surface to assist with flotation
options.d: All answers are correct

explanation: Trim weights are adjustable weights placed strategically (e.g., on the BC/back) to help achieve proper horizontal body trim.
source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

question: Why must o-rings be properly maintained and replaced if necessary?

options.a: They help the equipment move through the water
options.b: They provide additional buoyancy to the equipment
options.c: They regulate the speed breathing gas
options.d: They prevent water from entering equipment and keep the internal parts dry

explanation: O-rings seal equipment against water ingress, keeping internal parts dry; failure causes leaks and damage.
source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

question: How can waves affect diving conditions?

options.a: Larges waves in the surf zone can be hazardous
options.b: Choppy seas and rough conditions can make exits and entries difficult
options.c: All answers are correct
options.d: Wave action can reduce underwater visibility

explanation: Surf hazards, difficult entries/exits, and reduced visibility - all are ways waves affect diving.
source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

question: What is mass?

options.a: The force exerted on an object by gravity
options.b: The thermal energy of an amount of matter
options.c: The amount of matter in a given substance or object
options.d: The molecular movement in an object

explanation: Mass is the amount of matter in a given object/substance; weight is the gravitational force on that mass.
source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B
